serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 400 g (1 plate), providing around 550 kcal, 45 g protein, 30 g fat, and 25 g carbohydrate.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Fish, mixed species, cooked, dry heat (SR Legacy #15264)
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for 'Chili Fish' (Turkish-style baked/grilled fish with chili-tomato sauce) exists in USDA FoodData Central, Singapore HPB Foodo, or IMR Malaysia MyFCD. Nutrient values are estimated by combining USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for cooked white fish (dry heat, #15264) with proportional contributions from chili-tomato sauce components (tomatoes, bell peppers, chili paste, olive oil, garlic) drawn from USDA FoodData Central ingredient entries. Input record values (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central fish entries and found to be plausible. Values retained with medium confidence. Energy cross-check: protein 11.25g × 4 + fat 7.5g × 9 + carbohydrate 6.25g × 4 = 45 + 67.5 + 25 = 137.5 kcal — internally consistent.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — analogous estimate based on mixed fish dish with tomato-based sauce
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Turkish-style Chili Fish in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 45 (low) based on the analogous category of mixed fish dishes with tomato-based vegetable sauces. The dominant carbohydrate contributors are tomatoes, bell peppers, and chili paste, all of which have low GI values (tomatoes GI ~15, bell peppers GI ~15, chili paste GI ~30–40 per University of Sydney GI Database entries for similar foods). The high protein content (11.25 g/100 g) from lean fish further attenuates postprandial glucose response. Glycemic load per serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (45 × 25.0) / 100 = 11.25 (moderate GL for a 400 g serving providing 25 g carbohydrate).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 225 mg/100 g — borderline for hypertension management; potassium ~320 mg/100 g from fish, tomatoes, and bell peppers partially offsets sodium load
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request low-sodium chili sauce or homemade preparation, limit added salt, and keep total sodium intake from this dish below 300 mg/100 g; the omega-3 fatty acids from fish and MUFAs from olive oil support vascular health and may modestly reduce blood pressure.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (6.25 g/100 g) and high protein (11.25 g/100 g) from lean fish slow gastric emptying and blunt postprandial glucose response; sugar content is only 2.0 g/100 g
type2_diabetes.guidance: Chili Fish is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its low carbohydrate and high lean protein content; pair with a low-GI side such as brown rice or salad rather than white rice, and verify that commercial chili paste does not contain added sugar (some pastes contain 5–10 g sugar/100 g).

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Lean fish provides omega-3 fatty acids; saturated fat is low at 1.5 g/100 g; olive oil contributes heart-healthy MUFAs; cholesterol is moderate at 30 mg/100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Chili Fish is generally suitable for individuals with cardiovascular disease given its lean protein, low saturated fat, and beneficial MUFA and omega-3 fatty acid profile; sodium at 225 mg/100 g should be monitored and low-sodium sauce preparation is recommended to keep total sodium within heart-healthy limits.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Nightshade vegetables (tomatoes, bell peppers, chili) contain solanine and capsaicin which may trigger joint inflammation in RA-sensitive individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with active rheumatoid arthritis should monitor for joint flare after consuming this dish due to its nightshade content; if nightshades are tolerated, the olive oil and lean fish provide anti-inflammatory benefit, and substitution with a nightshade-free herb-lemon-olive oil sauce is recommended for RA-sensitive individuals.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chili Fish is a Turkish-style baked or grilled fish dish served in Singapore, featuring a whole or filleted white fish prepared with a spicy chili-tomato sauce made from chili paste, tomatoes, bell peppers, garlic, olive oil, and herbs. The dish is typically baked or grilled until the fish is tender and flaky, with the sauce caramelising around the fish to create a rich, spicy, and slightly tangy flavour profile. The texture is moist and flaky from the fish, with a bold, aromatic sauce that carries heat from the chili and sweetness from the tomatoes. Nutritionally, Chili Fish provides approximately 137.5 kcal per 100 g, with 11.25 g protein from lean fish, 7.5 g total fat (predominantly heart-healthy MUFAs from olive oil and omega-3 fatty acids from fish), and only 6.25 g carbohydrate, making it a low-carbohydrate, high-protein main course. Sodium is 225 mg/100 g, which is borderline for hypertension management. The dish is halal and gluten-free. It is clinically suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management due to its low carbohydrate and high lean protein content, and for cardiovascular health due to its low saturated fat and beneficial fatty acid profile. The nightshade content (tomatoes, bell peppers, chili) may be a concern for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis. A standard serving is approximately 400 g (1 plate), providing around 550 kcal. The dish is commonly found in Turkish restaurants and food delivery platforms in Singapore.
common_user_queries: How many calories in chili fish?
Is chili fish good for diabetics?
What is chili fish made of?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Turkish-style Chili Fish exists in USDA FoodData Central, Singapore HPB Foodo, or IMR Malaysia MyFCD. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy cooked white fish data combined with proportional chili-tomato sauce component contributions drawn from USDA FoodData Central ingredient entries. The input record values (ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were cross-checked against USDA data and found internally consistent (energy cross-check passes). Medium confidence is appropriate given the composite dish estimation basis.
notes: 1. Nutrient values estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy cooked white fish (dry heat, #15264) combined with chili-tomato sauce component data from USDA FoodData Central ingredient entries; no exact approved-source entry exists for this composite dish. Energy cross-check: 11.25g protein × 4 + 7.5g fat × 9 + 6.25g carbohydrate × 4 = 45 + 67.5 + 25 = 137.5 kcal — internally consistent. 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed negligible/trace for baked/grilled fish with olive oil-based sauce per USDA FoodData Central; no industrially produced trans fats expected in this preparation. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g (3.5 g) and polyunsaturated_fat_g (1.8 g) estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy fish fatty acid profiles and olive oil contribution; not directly reported in input record. 4. potassium_mg (320 mg) estimated from fish, tomato, and bell pepper contributions per USDA FoodData Central; not directly reported in input record. 5. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed micronutrient breakdown for this composite Turkish-style chili fish dish is not available as a single entry in USDA FoodData Central, Singapore HPB Foodo, or IMR Malaysia MyFCD as of 2024. 6. Glycemic index set to 45 (low GI) as an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for mixed fish dishes with tomato-based vegetable sauces; GI is numeric per Veritas protocol. Glycemic load per serving = (45 × 25.0) / 100 = 11.25 for a 400 g serving. 7. The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) was listed as supporting 'menu_listing' only and does not provide nutrient data; it is not used as a nutrient source. USDA FoodData Central is used as the approved nutrient source. 8. Cultural tags 'Kebab' and 'Shawarma' from input are not retained as they do not describe this dish; 'Turkish' is retained as the primary cuisine tag. 9. Multilingual names provided for Singaporean context (English, Chinese Simplified, Chinese Traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) as the dish is served in Singapore.
